California.d: Most populous US state (39M). Largest state economy ($3.6T GDP). Major spending areas: public welfare (Medi-Cal), education (UC/CSU systems), and transportation.

Texas.d: Second most populous state (30M). No state income tax. Rapid population growth. Major spending: education, highways, public welfare (Medicaid).

New York.d: Fourth most populous state (19.7M). Highest per-capita state/local spending. Major costs: Medicaid (largest state program), education, public employee retirement.

Florida.d: Third most populous state (22.2M). No state income tax. Fast-growing population. Spending driven by education, public welfare, and hurricane preparedness.

Illinois.d: Sixth most populous state (12.6M). Significant pension obligations. Major spending: education, public welfare, employee retirement systems.

Pennsylvania.d: Fifth most populous state (12.9M). Large public welfare and education budgets. Significant hospital and health spending.

Ohio.d: Seventh most populous state (11.7M). Major spending areas: education, public welfare (Medicaid), and corrections.

New Jersey.d: Eleventh most populous state (9.3M). Among highest property taxes nationally. Large education and public employee retirement spending.

Georgia.d: Eighth most populous state (10.9M). Rapidly growing metro Atlanta region. Spending focused on education, public welfare, and transportation.

North Carolina.d: Ninth most populous state (10.7M). Growing technology sector. Major spending: education, public welfare, highways.

Michigan.d: Tenth most populous state (10.0M). Auto industry heritage. Significant education, public welfare, and infrastructure spending.

Virginia.d: Twelfth most populous state (8.6M). Proximity to DC drives federal employment. Major spending: education, public welfare, transportation.

Washington.d: Thirteenth most populous state (7.7M). No state income tax. Tech industry hub. Large education and public welfare budgets.

Massachusetts.d: Sixteenth most populous state (7.0M). Major higher education and healthcare spending. High per-capita spending state.

Arizona.d: Fourteenth most populous state (7.3M). Fastest-growing states. Education, public welfare, and corrections are major spending areas.

Tennessee.d: Fifteenth most populous state (7.1M). No state income tax. Growing healthcare sector. Major spending: education, public welfare.

Maryland.d: Nineteenth most populous state (6.2M). High median household income. Major spending: education, public welfare, health.

Indiana.d: Seventeenth most populous state (6.8M). Manufacturing-heavy economy. Spending focused on education, public welfare, highways.

Minnesota.d: Twenty-second most populous state (5.7M). Progressive tax structure. High per-capita spending on education and health.

Missouri.d: Eighteenth most populous state (6.2M). Mix of urban (KC, STL) and rural. Major spending: education, public welfare, highways.

Wisconsin.d: Twentieth most populous state (5.9M). Strong manufacturing and dairy sectors. Major spending: education, public welfare.

Colorado.d: Twenty-first most populous state (5.8M). TABOR amendment constrains spending growth. Education, public welfare, highways.

South Carolina.d: Twenty-third most populous state (5.2M). Growing manufacturing sector. Spending focused on education, public welfare.

Alabama.d: Twenty-fourth most populous state (5.0M). Below-average per-capita spending. Education and public welfare dominate budget.

Louisiana.d: Twenty-fifth most populous state (4.6M). Oil/gas revenue dependent. Hurricane recovery costs. Education, public welfare, hospitals.

Kentucky.d: Twenty-sixth most populous state (4.5M). Coal transition economy. Education, public welfare, and corrections spending.

Oregon.d: Twenty-seventh most populous state (4.2M). No sales tax. Education, public welfare, and natural resources spending.

Oklahoma.d: Twenty-eighth most populous state (4.0M). Energy sector dependent. Education, public welfare, highways.

Connecticut.d: Twenty-ninth most populous state (3.6M). Highest per-capita income. Large pension obligations. Education, public welfare.

Iowa.d: Thirty-first most populous state (3.2M). Agriculture-based economy. Education and public welfare are primary spending areas.

Utah.d: Thirtieth most populous state (3.3M). Youngest median age. Fast population growth. Education is largest spending category.

Nevada.d: Thirty-second most populous state (3.1M). Tourism/gaming economy. No income tax. Education, public welfare, police.

Arkansas.d: Thirty-third most populous state (3.0M). Agriculture and retail. Education, public welfare, highways.

Mississippi.d: Thirty-fourth most populous state (2.9M). Lowest per-capita income. Education and public welfare dominate budget.

Kansas.d: Thirty-fifth most populous state (2.9M). Agriculture and aerospace. Education, public welfare, highways.

New Mexico.d: Thirty-sixth most populous state (2.1M). Federal labs and military bases. Education, public welfare, health.

Nebraska.d: Thirty-seventh most populous state (2.0M). Agriculture economy. Education is primary spending category.

Idaho.d: Thirty-eighth most populous state (1.9M). Fastest-growing state. Education, highways, public welfare.

West Virginia.d: Fortieth most populous state (1.8M). Coal transition. Education, public welfare, highways.

Hawaii.d: Fortieth most populous state (1.4M). Highest cost of living. Centralized state system (no independent school districts). Education, public welfare.

New Hampshire.d: Forty-first most populous state (1.4M). No income or sales tax. Property tax dependent. Education, public welfare.

Maine.d: Forty-second most populous state (1.4M). Aging population. Education, public welfare, highways.

Montana.d: Forty-third most populous state (1.1M). No sales tax. Natural resources and education spending.

Rhode Island.d: Forty-fourth most populous state (1.1M). Smallest state by area. Education, public welfare, employee retirement.

Delaware.d: Forty-fifth most populous state (1.0M). Corporate incorporation hub. Education, public welfare, health.

South Dakota.d: Forty-sixth most populous state (0.9M). No income tax. Agriculture economy. Education, highways.

North Dakota.d: Forty-seventh most populous state (0.8M). Oil boom economy. Education, highways, public welfare.

Alaska.d: Forty-eighth most populous state (0.7M). Oil revenue fund (PFD). Highest per-capita spending. Education, highways, natural resources.

District of Columbia.d: Federal district, not a state. Functions as both state and local government. Education, public welfare, police, fire.

Vermont.d: Forty-ninth most populous state (0.6M). Smallest state by population after Wyoming. Education, public welfare, highways.

Wyoming.d: Least populous state (0.6M). No income tax. Mineral extraction economy. Education, highways, natural resources.

Education.d: Total education spending including K-12, higher education, and other education programs. Typically the largest category of state & local spending (~30% of total).

Higher Education.d: State universities, community colleges, and other post-secondary institutions. Includes operating costs, faculty salaries, and capital projects.

Elementary & Secondary Education.d: K-12 public school spending including instruction, administration, student transportation, and school construction.

Other Education.d: Education spending not classified as higher education or K-12, including vocational education, special education programs, and education administration.

Libraries.d: Public library systems including operations, staffing, collections, and facilities.

Public Welfare.d: Medicaid, TANF (cash assistance), SNAP administration, and other means-tested benefit programs. Second-largest spending category after education.

Hospitals.d: Public hospital operations including university medical centers, county hospitals, and psychiatric facilities.

Health.d: Public health programs excluding hospitals: disease prevention, health inspections, mental health services, environmental health.

Employment Security.d: Administration of unemployment insurance programs, job placement services, and workforce development.

Veterans' Services.d: State-funded veterans programs including veterans homes, benefits administration, and memorial services.

Highways.d: Road construction, maintenance, snow removal, traffic management, and highway administration. Includes state highways and local roads.

Air Transportation.d: Public airport operations, maintenance, and capital improvements. Primarily funded by user fees and federal grants.

Parking Facilities.d: Public parking garages and lots operated by state or local governments.

Sea & Inland Port Facilities.d: Public port operations, maintenance, and capital improvements for maritime commerce.

Police Protection.d: State police/highway patrol and local police departments. Includes patrol, investigation, traffic enforcement, and crime labs.

Fire Protection.d: Fire departments including firefighting, fire prevention, emergency medical services (where fire-based), and fire inspections.

Correction.d: State prisons, local jails, juvenile detention, probation, and parole services.

Protective Inspection & Regulation.d: Building inspections, occupational licensing, consumer protection, food/drug inspections, and regulatory enforcement.

Natural Resources.d: Fish and game management, forestry, soil conservation, water resources, and other natural resource programs.

Parks & Recreation.d: State and local parks, recreation centers, swimming pools, playgrounds, and cultural/arts programs.

Housing & Community Development.d: Public housing, rental assistance, community development, urban renewal, and housing code enforcement.

Sewerage.d: Wastewater collection, treatment, and disposal systems operated by local governments.

Solid Waste Management.d: Garbage collection, recycling, landfill operations, and hazardous waste management.

Financial Administration.d: Tax collection, treasury management, auditing, budgeting, and financial reporting functions.

Judicial & Legal.d: Courts, prosecutors, public defenders, legal counsel, and judicial administration.

General Public Buildings.d: Construction and maintenance of government office buildings not assigned to specific functions.

Other Government Administration.d: General executive/legislative functions, planning, personnel, and other administrative activities.

Interest on General Debt.d: Interest payments on bonds and other borrowing by state and local governments. Excludes utility and insurance trust debt.

Miscellaneous Commercial Activities.d: Government-operated commercial enterprises not classified elsewhere (e.g., public golf courses, convention centers).

Other & Unallocable.d: Expenditures not classifiable to specific functions, including shared services and multi-purpose spending.

Utility Expenditure.d: Government-owned utilities: water, electric, gas, and transit systems. Largely self-funded through user charges.

Water Supply.d: Public water supply systems including treatment, distribution, and capital improvements.

Electric Power.d: Government-owned electric utilities including generation, transmission, and distribution.

Transit.d: Public transit systems including buses, subways, light rail, and commuter rail.

Liquor Store Expenditure.d: State-operated liquor stores in control states (17 states). Includes operations, inventory, and facilities.

Insurance Trust Expenditure.d: Public employee retirement systems, unemployment compensation, and workers' compensation. Largely funded by dedicated contributions.

Unemployment Compensation.d: State unemployment insurance benefit payments to eligible unemployed workers.

Employee Retirement.d: Pension benefits paid to retired state and local government employees. The largest insurance trust expenditure.

Workers' Compensation.d: Benefits for workers injured on the job, administered by state-operated workers' compensation programs.

Other Insurance Trust.d: Other insurance trust expenditures not classified as unemployment, retirement, or workers' compensation.
